title: Genesis AI debuts GENE-26.5, a foundation model for human-like robot manipulation
subtitle: The Khosla-backed startup pairs a dexterous hand and cheap data-collection glove with a foundation model trained to crack eggs, pipette samples, and run a 20-step recipe.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: On May 6, Genesis AI announced GENE-26.5, a foundation model the company calls the first robotic 'brain' capable of human-level physical manipulation. The release pairs three components: the model itself, a human-scale dexterous robotic hand, and a teleoperation glove the company says costs roughly one-hundredth of typical hardware while delivering up to five times the data-collection efficiency. Demo videos show two robotic hands cooking a 20-step meal — chopping tomatoes, cracking an egg one-handed, blending a smoothie, and pouring mid-air — and conducting lab procedures including pipetting and liquid transfer.

Robotic manipulation has been the slowest-moving frontier in AI. Vision and language models scaled with internet-sized datasets; manipulation has been bottlenecked by the cost of collecting real-robot demonstrations. Genesis is attacking that bottleneck on two sides: a cheap glove that lets humans generate demonstrations at scale, and a high-fidelity simulator the company says narrows the sim-to-real gap enough that policies trained in simulation transfer to physical hardware. The bet is that the data flywheel that worked for LLMs can finally work for hands.

Genesis raised a $105 million seed round backed by Eclipse, Khosla Ventures, Bpifrance, HSG, Eric Schmidt, Xavier Niel, and roboticists Daniela Rus and Vladlen Koltun. The release lands in a year where every major lab has a robotics push: Meta's Assured Robot Intelligence program for humanoids, SoftBank's Roze data-center robots, Nvidia and Cadence's sim-to-real toolchain, and Sony's table-tennis robot in Nature. The next milestone Genesis flagged is a general-purpose robot built on top of GENE-26.5 — the test of whether the foundation model approach generalizes beyond demo videos.

For learners: with manipulation models, the question to ask is not 'can the demo work' but 'can the system handle objects and environments the model has never seen.' Demo cuts are choreographed; the real benchmark is how a policy degrades when the kitchen layout, lighting, or ingredient changes. Watch for academic and third-party evaluations on out-of-distribution tasks before you draw conclusions about how close human-level dexterity actually is.

sources.title: Genesis AI Unveils GENE-26.5, the First AI Brain to Enable Robots with Human-Level Physical Manipulation Capabilities — PR Newswire
